questions.explanation: The correct answer, Option D, correctly uses the simple past tense 'had' in the conditional clause to express a current hypothetical situation: 'We might buy a larger house if we had more money.' The verb 'might buy' appropriately indicates a potential action based on this condition. Option A is incorrect because it uses 'buy' instead of the past participle 'bought.' Option B is incorrect because it combines a past perfect condition ('had had') with a present/future action ('might buy'), which is inconsistent. Option C is incorrect due to the missing article 'a,' but it does correctly use the tense for a present hypothetical.

questions.text: Identify the type of sentenceI didn’t know what time it was so I was very late to class.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A simple sentence has only one independent clause.
A compound sentence has two or more independent clauses joined by a conjunction but this sentence has only one independent clause.

The sentence contains:- One independent clause (i.e., a clause that could stand alone as a separate sentence):- "I was very late to class"- One dependent clause (i.e., a clause that cannot stand alone as a separate sentence):- "Because I didn't know what time it was" (implied) or "I didn't know what time it was" (explicit connected by "so")The dependent clause "I didn't know what time it was" provides background information and explains why the speaker was late to class. The word "so" connects the dependent clause to the independent clause indicating a cause and effect relationship.
A compound complex sentence has two or more independent clauses and one or more dependent clauses but this sentence has only one independent clause.

questions.explanation: The sentence contains:- One independent clause (i.e., a clause that could stand alone as a separate sentence):- "I was very late to class"- One dependent clause (i.e., a clause that cannot stand alone as a separate sentence):- "Because I didn't know what time it was" (implied) or "I didn't know what time it was" (explicit, connected by "so")The dependent clause "I didn't know what time it was" provides background information and explains why the speaker was late to class. The word "so" connects the dependent clause to the independent clause indicating a cause and effect relationship.

questions.text: Identify the type of sentenceThe doctor wants to prescribe physical therapy, and he wants me to see Dr. Smith whom he recommended.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A simple sentence has only one independent clause.
A compound sentence has two or more independent clauses joined by a conjunction, but this sentence has a dependent clause in addition to the independent clauses.
A complex sentence has one independent clause and one or more dependent clauses, but this sentence has two independent clauses in addition to the dependent clause.

The sentence contains:- Two independent clauses (i.e., clauses that could stand alone as separate sentences): - "The doctor wants to prescribe physical therapy" - "he wants me to see Dr. Smith"- A dependent clause (i.e., a clause that cannot stand alone as a separate sentence): - "whom he recommended" (this clause is a dependent clause because it starts with the pronoun "whom", which is the object of the verb "recommended")The sentence is compound because it contains two independent clauses joined by "and" complex because one of the independent clauses ("he wants me to see Dr. Smith") is modified by the dependent clause ("whom he recommended").

questions.explanation: The sentence contains:- Two independent clauses (i.e., clauses that could stand alone as separate sentences): - "The doctor wants to prescribe physical therapy" - "he wants me to see Dr. Smith"- A dependent clause (i.e., a clause that cannot stand alone as a separate sentence): - "whom he recommended" (this clause is a dependent clause because it starts with the pronoun "whom", which is the object of the verb "recommended")The sentence is compound because it contains two independent clauses joined by "and", and complex because one of the independent clauses ("he wants me to see Dr. Smith") is modified by the dependent clause ("whom he recommended").

questions.text: Identify the type of sentence Why he didn’t jump at the chance of a trip abroad I really can’t imagine.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A simple sentence has only one independent clause but this sentence has a dependent clause in addition to the independent clause.
A compound sentence has two or more independent clauses joined by a conjunction, but this sentence has only one independent clause.

The sentence contains:- One independent clause (i.e., a clause that could stand alone as a separate sentence): - "I really can't imagine"- One dependent clause (i.e., a clause that cannot stand alone as a separate sentence): - "Why he didn’t jump at the chance of a trip abroad" (this clause is a dependent clause because it starts with the word "why", which is a subordinating conjunction)The dependent clause "Why he didn’t jump at the chance of a trip abroad" is a subordinate clause that functions as the object of the verb "imagine". It provides the content of what is being imagined.
A compound complex sentence has two or more independent clauses and one or more dependent clauses but this sentence has only one independent clause.

questions.explanation: The sentence contains:- One independent clause (i.e., a clause that could stand alone as a separate sentence): - "I really can't imagine"- One dependent clause (i.e., a clause that cannot stand alone as a separate sentence): - "Why he didn’t jump at the chance of a trip abroad" (this clause is a dependent clause because it starts with the word "why", which is a subordinating conjunction)The dependent clause "Why he didn’t jump at the chance of a trip abroad" is a subordinate clause that functions as the object of the verb "imagine". It provides the content of what is being imagined.

The sentence gives a direct command or instruction, specifically telling someone not to remove luggage trolleys from the station. It's a clear directive, making it an imperative sentence.
An optative sentence expresses a wish or desire but this sentence gives a direct instruction.
An exclamatory sentence expresses strong emotions, but this sentence is a neutral command.

questions.explanation: The sentence gives a direct command or instruction specifically telling someone not to remove luggage trolleys from the station. It's a clear directive making it an imperative sentence.

questions.text: Demonstrate the control of tenses and sentence structure in the following sentence:This summer our vacation should be both exciting and restful.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A complex sentence is a sentence with one independent clause and at least one dependent clause. It works best when you need to provide more information to explain or modify your sentence's main point.

A simple sentence has the most basic elements that make it a sentence: a subject, a verb, and a completed thought. Examples of simple sentences include the following: Joe waited for the train. "Joe" = subject, "waited" = verb

A compound sentence is made up of two independent clauses joined by a coordinating conjunction (for, and, nor, but, or, yet, or so) and a comma or by a semicolon alone. Example: The pirate captain lost her treasure map, but she still found the buried treasure.

A compound-complex sentence is comprised of at least two independent clauses and one or more dependent clauses. Example: Though Mitchell prefers watching romantic films, he rented the latest spy thriller, and he enjoyed it very much.

questions.explanation: A simple sentence has the most basic elements that make it a sentence: a subject, a verb, and a completed thought. Examples of simple sentences include the following: Joe waited for the train. "Joe" = subject, "waited" = verb

questions.text: Demonstrate control of tenses and sentence structure.Choose the future indefinite tense form of the given sentence. "The parcel has been delivered.''
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: The sentence asks for the future indefinite tense form.But “had been delivered” is past perfect tense, not future.So it does not show a future action, which is why it’s incorrect.

Will is used to indicating future tense and the future of been is will be. The parcel will be delivered is in the future indefinite tense, which is used to describe an action that will happen in the future. In this sentence, the action of delivering the parcel is going to happen in the future. This is the correct answer.

Was delivered” is simple past passive, not future indefinite.The question specifically asks you to change the sentence into future indefinite tense, so only: "The parcel will ne delivered" is correct.

options.text: The parcel would have been delivered.
options.explanation: “Would have been delivered” is conditional perfect passive, not future indefinite.It talks about a hypothetical situation in the past — not a future action.

questions.explanation: Will is used to indicating future tense and the future of been is will be. The parcel will be delivered is in the future indefinite tense, which is used to describe an action that will happen in the future. In this sentence, the action of delivering the parcel is going to happen in the future. This is the correct answer.

This option is incorrect because it is in the infinitive form of the verb and does not specify the tense or subject.

questions.text: Identify the tense used in the given sentence:"You are always working on your laptop."
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: The present indefinite tense (simple present) uses the base form of the verb (e.g., you work). In the sentence “You are always working on your laptop”, the structure “are working” shows an ongoing action, so it is present continuous tense, not present indefinite.

The present perfect tense uses “have/has + past participle” (e.g., have worked). In the sentence “You are always working on your laptop”, the verb form “are working” is present continuous, not present perfect, so present perfect tense is not correct here.
The sentence uses “are working”, which is the present continuous form (am/is/are + verb-ing).It indicates an action happening repeatedly or around the present time.

The present perfect continuous tense uses the form “have/has been + verb-ing” (e.g., You have been working). In the sentence “You are always working on your laptop”, the structure “are working” lacks “have/has been”, so it is present continuous tense, not present perfect continuous.

questions.explanation: Present continuous tense is formed through the subject + present form of the verb (‘working’) + present participle of the verb (‘are’).Here, the given sentence that is 'You are always working on your Laptop' is the present tense.As we know by the rule of grammar, the definition of the present tense is the activity that is done in the current state not in the past or the future.The person seems to be always working on his/her laptop. This is a declarative sentence.The verb 'are working' is in the present continuous tense.Hence, the correct answer is present continuous tense.

questions.text: Demonstrate control of tenses and sentence structure:Fetch me a glass of water.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: “Fetch me a glass of water” is imperative, not declarative, because it gives a command or request. A declarative sentence states a fact or opinion, like “I am thirsty,” whereas this sentence directs someone to perform an action, which is why declarative is incorrect.
B is correct. A sentence in which an order is given or a request is made is called Imperative sentence.

It is imperative because it gives a direct command or request. It is not interrogative because it does not ask a question; it does not end with a question mark or seek information. The sentence instructs someone to act, which defines the imperative mood.

It is not exclamatory because it does not express strong emotion, surprise, or excitement. Exclamatory sentences usually end with an exclamation mark (!) and focus on the speaker’s feelings, whereas “Fetch me a glass of water” is simply a direct command, making it imperative.

questions.explanation: B is correct. A sentence in which an order is given or a request is made is called Imperative sentence.

questions.explanation: Note that await does not take the preposition 'for'. For example, we are awaiting his call. (We will not say: We are awaiting for his call). It will be 'waiting' instead of 'awaiting'.

questions.text: Find the error.A ship laden with merchandise got drowned in the Pacific Ocean.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: "A ship," is the subject of the sentence and introduces the noun that the sentence is about.
"Laden with merchandise," is a descriptive phrase that tells us more about the ship, specifically what it was carrying.

"Got drowned," is the verb phrase that tells us what happened to the ship. "Got drowned" is the past tense of "drown," which means to die by submerging in water. Ship is an inanimate object and thus, it cannot die. "Sunk" will be used instead of drown
"In the Pacific Ocean," is a prepositional phrase that tells us where the ship sank. It provides additional information about the location of the event.

questions.explanation: Option A, "A ship," is the subject of the sentence and introduces the noun that the sentence is about.Option B, "laden with merchandise," is a descriptive phrase that tells us more about the ship, specifically what it was carrying.Option C, "got drowned," is the verb phrase that tells us what happened to the ship. "Got drowned" is the past tense of "drown," which means to die by submerging in water.Drown is used for animate objects, ship is inanimate. It cannot die from drowning. "Sunk" will be used instead.Option D, "in the Pacific Ocean," is a prepositional phrase that tells us where the ship sank. It provides additional information about the location of the event.

questions.text: What part is wrong in the following sentence?You must had a kind and gentle heart if you want to become a successful doctor.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Had is the wrong verb form used in the sentence. Had is the second form of the word ‘have’. Since the tense used is simple present, so have would’ve made the sentence correct.NOTE: The verbs Had and Have mentioned here are used as the main verb. Do not confuse them with their use of helping verbs.
'A kind and gentle heart’ are general qualities associated with the profession. These are adjectives used to describe the heart and are grammatically correct.

‘If you want’ is the beginning of the second clause of the sentence, indicating the condition. This is also our clue to identify the tense of the sentence as want is the first form of the verb so the sentence must be in the present tense.
This is also, grammatically and sense-wise, the correct part of the sentence.

questions.explanation: Had is the wrong verb form used in the sentence. Had is the second form of the word ‘have’. Since the tense used is simple present, so have would’ve made the sentence correct.

questions.text: Each sentence below has a blank indicating that something has been omitted. Choose the word that best fits in with the meaning of the sentence as a whole.The robbers were caught because they had left _ fingerprints behind.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This is an object pronoun, used to indicate the recipient of an action, but it doesn't fit in this context.
This is a subject pronoun, used to indicate the performer of the action, but it doesn't fit in this context.

The correct answer is C. 'their'. The sentence should read: "The robbers were caught because they had left their fingerprints behind." The possessive adjective "their" indicates that the fingerprints belonged to the robbers.
This is a possessive pronoun, used to show ownership, but it's not necessary in this sentence.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is C. 'their'. The sentence should read: "The robbers were caught because they had left their fingerprints behind." The possessive adjective "their" indicates that the fingerprints belonged to the robbers.

questions.text: Each sentence below has a blank indicating that something has been omitted. Choose the word that best fits in with the meaning of the sentence as a whole.l asked two people the way to the station but _ of them knew.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This is a pronoun that indicates the absence of something, but it doesn't fit in this context.
This is a pronoun that indicates one or the other, but it doesn't fit in this context.
This is a pronoun that indicates two things together, but it's the opposite of what the sentence is trying to say.

The correct answer is D. 'neither'. The sentence should read: "I asked two people the way to the station, but neither of them knew." The word "neither" indicates that none of the two people knew the way to the station.

questions.text: The last thing that the fond mother gave her only son was _ blessing.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: "His" would imply that the blessing belongs to the son, which is not the case.
- "Her" is the correct possessive pronoun to use, as it shows that the blessing belongs to the mother.- The sentence is saying that the mother gave her own blessing to her son.
"Hers" is a possessive pronoun, but it's not the correct choice here, as it would imply that the blessing is already in the son's possession.
"Their" would imply that the blessing belongs to both the mother and the son, which is not the intended meaning

questions.explanation: - "Her" is the correct possessive pronoun to use, as it shows that the blessing belongs to the mother.- The sentence is saying that the mother gave her own blessing to her son.

questions.text: Governments and business houses must reduce _ own energy use and promote conservation to their citizens and employees.

options.explanation: "Its" would imply that the energy use belongs to a single entity, rather than both governments and business houses.
Them" is an object pronoun, not a possessive pronoun, so it doesn't fit the context.

"Their" is the correct possessive pronoun to use, as it shows that the energy use belongs to both governments and business houses.- The sentence is saying that both governments and business houses must take responsibility for reducing their own energy consumption and promoting conservation.
"His" would imply that the energy use belongs to a male individual, which is not the intended meaning.

questions.explanation: "Their" is the correct possessive pronoun to use, as it shows that the energy use belongs to both governments and business houses.- The sentence is saying that both governments and business houses must take responsibility for reducing their own energy consumption and promoting conservation.

"All of that" would imply that the speaker is referring to a specific group or selection of dresses, rather than all of them.
"All of this" would imply that the speaker is referring to a general situation or circumstance, rather than the specific dresses.

questions.explanation: All of them" is the correct pronoun to use, as it refers back to the plural noun "dresses".- The sentence is saying that the speaker bought every single dress, because they all looked good.

questions.text: They live in a _ house.

questions.text: What is the synonym of the word PROTRACTION?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Protraction means to extend or prolong something in time, space, or scope. Prolongation is a noun that means the act of prolonging or extending something, often in duration or length.
Embark on means to start or begin a journey, project, or venture. It's unrelated to the meaning of protraction.
Cacophony refers to a harsh, discordant sound or a lack of harmony. It's unrelated to the meaning of protraction.
Divagate means to wander or stray from a path or topic, often in a vague or aimless way. It's antonymous to protraction, as it implies a lack of focus or direction.

questions.explanation: Option A is correct.Protraction means to extend or prolong something in time, space, or scope. Prolongation is a noun that means the act of prolonging or extending something, often in duration or length.

questions.text: What is the synonym of the word PSEUDO?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Pseudo means false, pretended, or imitation. Quasi is a prefix that means "resembling" or "having some, but not all, of the characteristics of". It's often used to describe something that is pseudo or pretended.
Genuine means authentic, real, or sincere. It's antonymous to pseudo, as it implies the opposite of false or pretended.
Exhilarating means extremely exciting or thrilling. It's antonymous to pseudo, as it implies authenticity and genuineness.
Fluffy means soft, light, and airy, often used to describe something pleasant but lacking substance. While it's not directly related to pseudo, it doesn't share the same meaning either.

questions.explanation: Option A is correct.Pseudo means false, pretended, or imitation. Quasi is a prefix that means "resembling" or "having some, but not all, of the characteristics of". It's often used to describe something that is pseudo or pretended.

questions.text: What is the synonym of the word PUTRID?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Witty means clever, humorous, or amusing. It's antonymous to putrid, as it implies something pleasant and engaging.
Shaggy means having a rough, unkempt, or hairy appearance. While it might describe something unpleasant, it doesn't convey the same meaning as putrid.
Florid means elaborate, ornate, or excessively decorative. It's unrelated to the meaning of putrid.
Putrid means rotting, decaying, or having a foul smell. Rancid is an adjective that means having a strong, unpleasant smell or taste, often due to spoilage or decay.

questions.explanation: Option D is correct.Putrid means rotting, decaying, or having a foul smell. Rancid is an adjective that means having a strong, unpleasant smell or taste, often due to spoilage or decay.

questions.text: What is the antonym of ﻿EXCURSION?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Farrago – A confused mixture; unrelated.Stick around – To stay or remain; opposite in meaning.
Expedition – A short journey or trip; same meaning.
Exacerbation – Making something worse; unrelated.

Excursion means a trip or journey away from a place. Stick around means to stay or remain in a place, which is the opposite of Excursion.They are antonyms, or words with opposite meanings. While Excursion implies movement away from a place, Stick around implies staying in the same place.

questions.explanation: Option D is correct.Excursion means a trip or journey away from a place. Stick around means to stay or remain in a place, which is the opposite of Excursion.They are antonyms, or words with opposite meanings. While Excursion implies movement away from a place, Stick around implies staying in the same place.

questions.text: What is the antonym of ﻿EXAGGERATION?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Hyperbole – Overstatement; same meaning.
Obfuscation – Confusion or obscuring; unrelated.

Exaggeration means to enlarge or magnify something beyond its true size, importance, or degree. Belittlement means to make something seem smaller or less important, which is the opposite of exaggeration.They are antonyms, or words with opposite meanings. While Exaggeration implies making something seem bigger, Belittlement implies making something seem smaller.
Peregrination – Long journey; unrelated.

questions.explanation: Option D is correct.Exaggeration means to enlarge or magnify something beyond its true size, importance, or degree. Belittlement means to make something seem smaller or less important, which is the opposite of exaggeration.They are antonyms, or words with opposite meanings. While Exaggeration implies making something seem bigger, Belittlement implies making something seem smaller.

questions.text: What is the synonyms of ﻿FLICKED?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Supplicated – Begged or pleaded; unrelated.
Extirpated – Destroyed completely; unrelated
Zipped – Moved quickly; same sense.

Flicked means to move or strike something with a quick, sudden motion, often releasing or unwinding something. Unwound means to unwind or uncoil something, or to relax and become less tense.In this case, Flicked and Unwound are related in the sense that a flicking motion can be used to unwind or release something.

questions.explanation: Option D is correct.Flicked means to move or strike something with a quick, sudden motion, often releasing or unwinding something. Unwound means to unwind or uncoil something, or to relax and become less tense.

questions.text: Identify the type of Phrase:Tom visited India while studying the history of Indian art.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: An appositive phrase renames or explains a noun. This phrase does not rename or explain a noun.
An infinitive phrase begins with an infinitive (a verb form beginning with "to" or a verb form without "to" in a subordinate clause). This phrase begins with "while", not an infinitive.
: A noun phrase functions as a noun. This phrase functions as an adverb, not a noun.

The phrase "while studying the history of Indian art" is a prepositional phrase, which is a phrase that begins with a preposition (in this case, "while") and functions as a noun, adjective, or adverb.- In this case, the prepositional phrase "while studying the history of Indian art" functions as an adverb, modifying the verb "visited" and indicating the circumstances under which Tom visited India.

questions.explanation: The phrase "while studying the history of Indian art" is a prepositional phrase, which is a phrase that begins with a preposition (in this case, "while") and functions as a noun, adjective, or adverb.- In this case, the prepositional phrase "while studying the history of Indian art" functions as an adverb, modifying the verb "visited" and indicating the circumstances under which Tom visited India.
